title: myTax 2026 Foreign income tax offset
description: How to claim the foreign income tax offset when lodging your tax return using myTax.
keywords: 2026, myTax, tax offset, attribution account payment

content_blocks.text: Complete this section if you paid foreign tax on:

items: income you included in your assessable income this year

an attribution account payment you received (usually a dividend distribution) that was paid out of previously attributed income and that payment is non-assessable non-exempt income. This includes where you acquired employee share scheme interests at a discount in relation to your foreign employment paid tax on the discounts in the foreign country.

content_blocks.text: You may be entitled to the foreign income tax offset (FITO). FITO is a non-refundable offset.

The Guide to foreign income tax offset rules explains which foreign taxes count towards the offset. If you received a distribution statement from a managed fund that reports a ‘Foreign tax offset applicable to discountable capital gains’ you'll need to apportion that amount.
You need to read the Guide to foreign income tax offset rules , if either:

items: the total amount of foreign tax you paid during 2025–26 exceeds $1,000

you paid foreign tax on an attribution account payment you received (usually a dividend distribution) that was paid out of previously attributed income and that payment is non-assessable non-exempt income

the amount of foreign tax you paid relates to an amount that differs from the amount you included in your assessable income. For example, where you have both capital losses and foreign capital gains, the net capital gain you included in your assessable income will be less than the foreign capital gain on which you paid foreign tax.

content_blocks.text: For Australian resident individuals with Joint Petroleum Development Area (JPDA) income, the FITO for an employee is the lesser of:

items: Australian tax payable on the net assessable JPDA income (JPDA income less allowable deductions relating to that income)
Timor-Leste tax paid on JPDA employment income.

content_blocks.text: The FITO limit doesn't apply to this income.
You must convert all foreign tax paid to Australian dollars before you enter your amounts into your tax return. Use the Foreign income conversion calculator This link opens in a new window .
At Personalise return , you don’t need to make a selection to show FITO. It's always shown at Prepare return when you have shown an amount at foreign tax paid in the following sections:
To show your FITO, at Prepare return select 'Add/Edit' at the Offsets banner.
At the Foreign income tax offset heading:

items: Does the total amount of the foreign tax you paid during 2025–26 exceed $1,000? No – go to step 2. Yes – go to step 3.

When the total amount of the foreign tax you paid during 2025–26 doesn't exceed $1,000, the amount of foreign tax you paid is automatically completed at Offset amount in the Offsets section. Go to step 4.

When the total amount of the foreign tax you paid during 2025–26 exceeds $1,000, you need to work out the full amount of FITO you can claim and enter it at Offset amount . To work out the total FITO you can claim, see Guide to foreign income tax offset rules . Alternatively, you can simply enter a tax offset amount of $1,000 at Offset amount . However, if you claim only $1,000 for 2025–26, you won't be able to claim the rest of your 2025–26 foreign tax in a future income year.
Select Save and continue when you have completed the Offsets section.

content_blocks.text: Note: If you paid foreign tax on exempt foreign employment income and included the amount in your tax return at the Foreign employment section, your FITO amount may be different on your notice of assessment. We may calculate your FITO amount in this circumstance.

content_text:
## Things to know
Complete this section if you paid foreign tax on:
- income you included in your assessable income this year
- an attribution account payment you received (usually a dividend distribution) that was paid out of previously attributed income and that payment is non-assessable non-exempt income. This includes where you acquired employee share scheme interests at a discount in relation to your foreign employment paid tax on the discounts in the foreign country.
You may be entitled to the foreign income tax offset (FITO). FITO is a non-refundable offset.
The Guide to foreign income tax offset rules explains which foreign taxes count towards the offset. If you received a distribution statement from a managed fund that reports a ‘Foreign tax offset applicable to discountable capital gains’ you'll need to apportion that amount.
You need to read the Guide to foreign income tax offset rules , if either:
- the total amount of foreign tax you paid during 2025–26 exceeds $1,000
- you paid foreign tax on an attribution account payment you received (usually a dividend distribution) that was paid out of previously attributed income and that payment is non-assessable non-exempt income
- the amount of foreign tax you paid relates to an amount that differs from the amount you included in your assessable income. For example, where you have both capital losses and foreign capital gains, the net capital gain you included in your assessable income will be less than the foreign capital gain on which you paid foreign tax.
For Australian resident individuals with Joint Petroleum Development Area (JPDA) income, the FITO for an employee is the lesser of:
- Australian tax payable on the net assessable JPDA income (JPDA income less allowable deductions relating to that income)
- Timor-Leste tax paid on JPDA employment income.
The FITO limit doesn't apply to this income.
## Completing this section
You must convert all foreign tax paid to Australian dollars before you enter your amounts into your tax return. Use the Foreign income conversion calculator This link opens in a new window .
At Personalise return , you don’t need to make a selection to show FITO. It's always shown at Prepare return when you have shown an amount at foreign tax paid in the following sections:
- Income statements and payment summaries
- Managed fund and trust distributions
- Foreign income, assets and entities
- Other income
To show your FITO, at Prepare return select 'Add/Edit' at the Offsets banner.
At the Foreign income tax offset heading:
- Does the total amount of the foreign tax you paid during 2025–26 exceed $1,000? No – go to step 2. Yes – go to step 3.
- When the total amount of the foreign tax you paid during 2025–26 doesn't exceed $1,000, the amount of foreign tax you paid is automatically completed at Offset amount in the Offsets section. Go to step 4.
- When the total amount of the foreign tax you paid during 2025–26 exceeds $1,000, you need to work out the full amount of FITO you can claim and enter it at Offset amount . To work out the total FITO you can claim, see Guide to foreign income tax offset rules . Alternatively, you can simply enter a tax offset amount of $1,000 at Offset amount . However, if you claim only $1,000 for 2025–26, you won't be able to claim the rest of your 2025–26 foreign tax in a future income year.
- Select Save and continue when you have completed the Offsets section.
Note: If you paid foreign tax on exempt foreign employment income and included the amount in your tax return at the Foreign employment section, your FITO amount may be different on your notice of assessment. We may calculate your FITO amount in this circumstance.
source_title: myTax 2026 Foreign income tax offset - Australian Taxation Office
source_description: How to claim the foreign income tax offset when lodging your tax return using myTax. Published 1 June 2026.
